principle: Use the most obvious conventional label for every navigation item and button — clever or branded names make users think
explanation: X.ux|obvious_labels: users_scan_not_read -> label_must_be_instantly_recognized; conventional_beats_clever: "Jobs" >> "Job-o-Rama" + "Employment_Opportunities"; "Sign_in" >> "Access_Your_Account"; "Cart" >> "My_Shopping_Experience"; every_unclear_label == question_mark_in_user_head == cognitive_load; NO:marketing_names_for_nav NO:branded_jargon_for_common_actions NO:clever_wordplay_on_buttons eg: "Job-o-Rama + Employment_Opportunities" >> "Jobs"

principle: Break visual or structural consistency when it makes the interface significantly clearer — clarity always wins over uniformity
explanation: X.ux|clarity_over_consistency: consistency == good_default BUT NOT absolute_rule; if_slightly_inconsistent == significantly_clearer -> choose_clarity; eg: form_with_one_wider_field_breaking_grid BUT making_purpose_obvious >> all_fields_same_width_but_confusing; applies_to: nav_item_styled_differently_to_signal_current_section + button_breaking_color_pattern_for_emphasis; NO:blind_consistency_that_hides_meaning NO:same_style_when_different_behavior eg: "all_nav_items_identical_styling" >> "current_section_visually_distinct_even_if_inconsistent"
